variables.content: The sequence consisting of (i) ab initio DFPT evaluation of long-wavelength phonons, Born charges, and dielectric response and computation of the long-wavelength Fröhlich vertex and its energy-resolved squared coupling density d(g^{2})/d\\omega to obtain the effective single-mode parameters \\tilde{g}_{\\mathrm{LO}}^{2} and \\hbar\\tilde{\\omega}_{\\mathrm{LO}}, and (ii) a Feynman-polaron calculation using the ab initio bare mass m_{0} and the Fröhlich parameter \\alpha computed from those inputs, reproduces the ARPES-measured hole effective mass m_{\\mathrm{exp}}=0.26\\pm0.02\\,m_e for orthorhombic CsPbBr$_3$ within experimental uncertainty; therefore this ab initio electron–phonon evaluation combined with a Feynman-polaron treatment is a viable predictive method to compute carrier effective masses in lead-halide perovskites when the Fröhlich mechanism dominates carrier dressing.

The effective mass entering the Drude prefactor used to infer transport parameters for photogenerated carriers in CsPbBr3 is taken as m_p = 3.5 × m*, where m_p is the effective polaron mass and m* = 0.21 m_e is the bare electronic band effective mass (m_e = 9.10938356×10^−31 kg is the electron rest mass); the multiplicative factor 3.5 is adopted from Feynman–Osaka polaron model estimates to account for polaron mass enhancement, and using this fixed scaling produces a physically appropriate value of m_p for the photogenerated carrier population in the studied nanocrystal thin films such that the uncertainty in this adopted scaling is small compared with the quoted mobility estimate μ = q τ_s / m_p.

Applying the finite-temperature Feynman–Ōsaka variational path-integral polaron formalism—parameterized by (i) the Fröhlich coupling constant $\\alpha_{\\mathrm{e-ph}}$, (ii) an effective carrier mass $m$, (iii) a characteristic LO phonon angular frequency $\\omega$, and (iv) the temperature $T$—and numerically minimizing the polaron free-energy functional $F(v,w;\\alpha,\\beta)$ with respect to the variational parameters $v$ and $w$ (which have units of angular frequency) yields a reduced mass for the relative motion $m_{\\mathrm{red}}=m\\frac{v^{2}-w^{2}}{v^{2}}$, a Feynman polaron radius $\\rho=\\sqrt{\\frac{3\\hbar}{2 m_{\\mathrm{red}} v 2\\pi\\omega}}$, and a finite-temperature mobility $\\mu$ via the Hellwarth–Feynman expressions; these computed $\\rho$ and $\\mu$ provide quantitatively meaningful estimates of the polaron radius and mobility in $CH_{3}NH_{3}PbBr_{3}$ and $CsPbBr_{3}$ provided the materials lie in the large-to-intermediate polaron regime ($\\alpha_{\\mathrm{e-ph}}\\sim1$–3) and provided that multimode, highly anharmonic, or disorder-induced scattering channels do not dominate transport beyond the single-mode Fröhlich picture.
      Miyata et al., Materials and Methods: Estimation of Fröhlich coupling and mobility; table S3

ARPES single-particle spectral-function measurements at finite experimental energy resolution produce a quasiparticle peak whose linewidth is determined by combined instrumental broadening, thermal disorder, and intrinsic many-body scattering; in CsPbBr$_3$ the relevant optical longitudinal-optical (LO) phonon energies are \\leq25\\ \\mathrm{meV} and phonon-replica satellites separated by these energies cannot be resolved because the experimental quasiparticle linewidth exceeds the mode energy separations, yet the ARPES-measured quasiparticle dispersion and the fitted increase in effective mass still faithfully reflect the quasiparticle mass renormalization due to electron–phonon interactions, so that extraction of an increased effective mass from the fitted dispersion constitutes a valid signature of polaronic mass dressing even in the absence of resolved replica peaks.

The momentum-resolved valence-band dispersion of single-crystalline orthorhombic CsPbBr$_3$ at room temperature was measured by angle-resolved photoelectron spectroscopy (ARPES); here the measured quasiparticle energy as a function of the in-plane crystal momentum was fitted near the valence-band maximum (VBM) by the isotropic in-plane parabolic dispersion E(k)=E_{\\mathrm{VBM}}+\\hbar^{2}k^{2}/(2m_{\\mathrm{exp}}), where k is the magnitude of the two-dimensional in-plane wave vector (units Å^{-1}), \\hbar is the reduced Planck constant, and m_e is the free electron mass, and the energy zero was set at the VBM defined as the energy of the maximum of the energy-distribution curve (EDC) at the band top; this fit, performed on the upper valence band along the surface high-symmetry direction labeled \\bar{\\Gamma}-\\bar{M}, returns an experimental hole effective mass m_{\\mathrm{exp}}=0.26\\pm0.02\\,m_e.

The charge-carrier large (Fröhlich) polaron in methylammonium lead iodide (CH3NH3PbI3, hereafter MAPI) at temperature $T=300\,$K has finite-temperature Feynman-variational internal parameters and spatial extents obtained from a numerical solution of the temperature-dependent continuum Fröhlich polaron model as follows: for the electron polaron the variational oscillator parameters are $v=19.88$ and $w=16.98$ (dimensionless numbers expressed in units of $\hbar\omega_{0}$ where $\omega_{0}$ is the effective optical-phonon angular frequency), giving a reduced polaron mass $\mu$ defined by $\mu = m(v^{2}-w^{2})/v^{2}$ (where $m$ is the bare-band effective mass used in the continuum model, for the electron $m_{e}=0.12\,m_{0}$ with $m_{0}$ the free-electron mass), and a root-mean-square polaron radius $r_{e}=\sqrt{\langle\rho^{2}\rangle}=(3/2\mu v)^{1/2}=26.8\ \mathring{\mathrm{A}}$; for the hole polaron the corresponding radius is $r_{h}=25.3\ \mathring{\mathrm{A}}$ with $v=20.11$ and $w=16.84$ (using a hole bare-band effective mass $m_{h}=0.15\,m_{0}$ in the same $\mu$ relation). The polaron free energies (variational free-energy minima of the Feynman functional) at $300\,$K are $F_{e}=35.5\ \text{meV}$ for the electron polaron and $F_{h}=43.6\ \text{meV}$ for the hole polaron. Under the weak-coupling, athermal Feynman approximation (small $\alpha$ expansion with $w\approx3$ and $v\approx3+\tfrac{2}{5}\alpha$) the same model yields an athermal estimate $r\approx29.5\ \mathring{\mathrm{A}}$, and the full finite-temperature variational solution produces a polaron radius that decreases with increasing temperature, as above.

Using continuum Fröhlich and Feynman variational formalisms to estimate large‑polaron parameters yields representative polaron radii for CsPbBr3 of electron ρ_e ≈ 26.7 Å and hole ρ_h ≈ 24.9 Å, and for representative organic perovskites larger electron polaron radii such as ρ_e ≈ 41.8 Å; for colloidal CsPbBr3 nanocrystals with physical size ≈15 nm and organic surface ligands, these continuum polaron radius estimates are taken as valid approximations for the screening length scale that controls dynamic Coulomb screening of interactions mediating carrier–LO‑phonon scattering, and smaller polaron radii correspond to reduced dynamic screening and therefore to stronger effective carrier–LO coupling in the nanocrystals under the stated experimental excitation conditions.

In room-temperature angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (ARPES) measurements on freshly cleaved single crystals of CsPbBr3, the hole effective mass at the valence-band maximum (VBM) located at the bulk Brillouin-zone R point was determined by fitting local quadratic dispersions and found to be m_{h}^{*}=0.203\pm0.016\,m_{0}, where m_{0} denotes the free-electron mass; this reported value is the average of four independent measurements on four different cleaved samples that produced individual fitted masses of 0.19\,m_{0}, 0.23\,m_{0}, 0.20\,m_{0}, and 0.19\,m_{0}. The identification of the VBM at the bulk R point was made by photon-energy-dependent ARPES mapping of the perpendicular crystal momentum k_{\perp}, and under the free-electron final-state mapping k_{\perp}=\sqrt{\tfrac{2m_{0}}{\hbar^{2}}\bigl(E_{\mathrm{kin}}\cos^{2}\theta + V_{0}\bigr)} with inner potential parameter V_{0}=5.3\ \mathrm{eV} (where \hbar is the reduced Planck constant, E_{\mathrm{kin}} the photoelectron kinetic energy referenced to the vacuum level, and \theta the electron emission polar angle), the first R point in these measurements corresponds to photon energy h\nu=29\ \mathrm{eV}.

Cesium lead bromide (CsPbBr_{3}) undergoes crystallographic phase changes with temperature and adopts an orthorhombic ground-state structure at room temperature, yet ARPES band dispersions measured at room temperature can in practice resemble higher-symmetry cubic-phase dispersions for some momentum-space cuts; therefore using an orthorhombic-structure G_{0}W_{0} calculation that yields m_{h}^{*}=0.226\,m_{0} for direct quantitative comparison with room-temperature ARPES data assumes that the orthorhombic theoretical band structure is an appropriate representation of the electronic states probed in the ARPES experiment, and further assumes that any difference in structural symmetry between the theoretical model and the actual electronic-state symmetry sampled by ARPES does not change the effective mass by an amount comparable to the experimental–theoretical difference being evaluated.

Applying the Feynman variational polaron-model weak-to-intermediate coupling expansion
      m_{\\mathrm{pol}}=m_{0}\\left(1+\\frac{\\alpha}{6}+0.025\\alpha^{2}+\\cdots\\right),
      with the inputs m_{0}=0.17\\,m_e and \\alpha=1.81 produces a polaron-renormalized hole mass m_{\\mathrm{pol}}=0.24\\,m_e; this theoretical mass renormalization reduces the discrepancy between the computed bare mass and the ARPES-derived mass to within the experimental uncertainty of \\pm0.02\\,m_e.
